title: LAMOL: LAnguage MOdeling for Lifelong Language Learning

structured.summary: LAMOL proposes a lifelong learning framework for NLP that unifies task solving and pseudo-sample generation within a single language model, effectively mitigating catastrophic forgetting without storing old data or expanding model capacity. Experiments on diverse NLP tasks demonstrate that LAMOL outperforms standard continual learning baselines and approaches multitask learning performance, establishing a competitive baseline for lifelong language learning.

strengths.point: Novel integration of task solving and pseudo-sample generation in one LM
strengths.evidence: LAMOL uses a single GPT-2 model to both answer questions and generate pseudo samples from prior tasks, avoiding extra generator modules or memory buffers.

strengths.point: Strong empirical performance across heterogeneous NLP tasks
strengths.evidence: LAMOL significantly outperforms fine-tuning, regularization-based methods, and data replay baselines like GEM, nearly matching multitask learning upper bound on five diverse NLP tasks.

strengths.point: Effective use of task-specific tokens to mitigate sample dilution
strengths.evidence: Introducing task-specific generation tokens improves stability and performance when the number of tasks grows, as shown in experiments on pseudo-sample ratios and multiple tasks.

strengths.point: Practical approach that requires no storage of past data or prior knowledge of total tasks
strengths.evidence: LAMOL can incrementally add tasks without expanding model size or storing exemplars, addressing key challenges in lifelong learning.

strengths.point: Comprehensive experimental analysis including forward transfer and pseudo-sample ratio effects
strengths.evidence: The paper studies how generated sample quantity affects performance and shows positive forward transfer in related tasks.

weaknesses.point: Insufficient comparison to prior generative replay work in NLP
weaknesses.evidence: The paper does not adequately discuss or contrast LAMOL with earlier generative replay methods applied to NLP, weakening novelty claims.

weaknesses.point: Lack of ablation on critical hyperparameter λ balancing QA and generation loss
weaknesses.evidence: No experiments analyze sensitivity to λ, leaving unclear how tuning affects performance.

weaknesses.point: No analysis of the quality or diversity of generated pseudo-samples
weaknesses.evidence: The paper omits evaluations on linguistic or semantic correctness of generated samples, limiting understanding of why pseudo replay helps.

weaknesses.point: Evaluation lacks unified lifelong learning metrics and task difficulty analysis
weaknesses.evidence: Only task-specific metrics are reported, and robustness across task complexity is not examined.

weaknesses.point: Baselines do not include more recent or stronger memory replay methods beyond GEM
weaknesses.evidence: Stronger exemplar selection or replay approaches post-ICLR 2020 are not considered, possibly overestimating LAMOL’s advantages.

weaknesses.point: Reproducibility hampered by missing details on pseudo-sample generation settings and training schedules
weaknesses.evidence: Key parameters like number of generated samples per iteration, decoding temperature, batch size, and epochs are not fully specified.

weaknesses.point: Clarity issues in explaining task token mechanisms and training losses
weaknesses.evidence: Descriptions of sample dilution and when QA vs generation loss apply are ambiguous or lack illustrative examples.

weaknesses.point: Limited assessment of generality across model architectures and languages
weaknesses.evidence: Only GPT-2 (decoder-only, English) is evaluated; no experiments on encoder-decoder LMs or multilingual tasks.

weaknesses.point: No evaluation on scalability to large numbers of tasks
weaknesses.evidence: Experiments cover up to 5–8 tasks; lifelong learning scenarios with dozens or hundreds of tasks remain untested.

weaknesses.point: Assumption that all NLP tasks can be cast as QA is not critically examined
weaknesses.evidence: Potential loss of structure or semantics in reformulating diverse tasks as QA is not discussed.

questions.question: How sensitive is LAMOL’s performance to the hyperparameter λ balancing QA and generation loss?
questions.motivation: Understanding this would clarify the robustness of training and guide practitioners in tuning the model.

questions.question: What is the quality, diversity, and correctness of the generated pseudo-samples, and how do these factors affect forgetting prevention?
questions.motivation: Evaluating sample quality is essential to ascertain whether improvements stem from meaningful replay or mere memorization.

questions.question: Can LAMOL’s approach generalize to encoder-decoder architectures or multilingual lifelong learning scenarios?
questions.motivation: Extending applicability would increase impact and relevance across the broader NLP community.

questions.question: How does LAMOL perform when scaling to many more tasks, e.g., dozens or hundreds, in terms of stability and performance?
questions.motivation: Lifelong learning demands scalability; evaluating limits is key for real-world deployment.

questions.question: Is the assumption that all tasks can be reframed as QA limiting, and are there tasks where this formulation degrades performance?
questions.motivation: Testing this assumption ensures the method's applicability is well understood.

critique_points.point_001: Insufficient comparison to prior generative replay work in NLP. The paper does not adequately discuss or contrast LAMOL with earlier generative replay methods applied to NLP, weakening novelty claims
critique_points.point_002: Lack of ablation on critical hyperparameter λ balancing QA and generation loss. No experiments analyze sensitivity to λ, leaving unclear how tuning affects performance
critique_points.point_003: No analysis of the quality or diversity of generated pseudo-samples. The paper omits evaluations on linguistic or semantic correctness of generated samples, limiting understanding of why pseudo replay helps
critique_points.point_004: Evaluation lacks unified lifelong learning metrics and task difficulty analysis. Only task-specific metrics are reported, and robustness across task complexity is not examined
critique_points.point_005: Baselines do not include more recent or stronger memory replay methods beyond GEM. Stronger exemplar selection or replay approaches post-ICLR 2020 are not considered, possibly overestimating LAMOL’s advantages
critique_points.point_006: Reproducibility hampered by missing details on pseudo-sample generation settings and training schedules. Key parameters like number of generated samples per iteration, decoding temperature, batch size, and epochs are not fully specified
critique_points.point_007: Clarity issues in explaining task token mechanisms and training losses. Descriptions of sample dilution and when QA vs generation loss apply are ambiguous or lack illustrative examples
critique_points.point_008: Limited assessment of generality across model architectures and languages. Only GPT-2 (decoder-only, English) is evaluated; no experiments on encoder-decoder LMs or multilingual tasks
critique_points.point_009: No evaluation on scalability to large numbers of tasks. Experiments cover up to 5–8 tasks; lifelong learning scenarios with dozens or hundreds of tasks remain untested
critique_points.point_010: Assumption that all NLP tasks can be cast as QA is not critically examined. Potential loss of structure or semantics in reformulating diverse tasks as QA is not discussed
